title: Getting Started with NVDA
description: Learn the essential NVDA commands every beginner needs. Each lesson is a short drill that takes about one to three minutes.

steps.instruction: Welcome to NVDA Coach. Before you practice any commands, it helps to understand something that most documentation glosses over: when you press a key on your keyboard, who is actually doing the work? There are three different layers that control keyboard commands on Windows, and knowing which layer a command belongs to will help you learn faster and make fewer mistakes. This lesson covers all three. Press Enter to continue.

The first layer is Windows itself. Some commands belong entirely to the operating system. Cut, Copy, and Paste — Control+X, Control+C, and Control+V — are Windows commands. When any application lets you paste text, it is not the application doing the pasting; it is making a request to Windows to do it. Opening the Start Menu, switching between windows with Alt+Tab, and closing a window with Alt+F4 are also Windows commands. They work the same way in every program because Windows itself is in charge. Press Enter to continue.

The second layer is the application you are using. When you press Down Arrow in a word processor, you are telling the word processor to move your cursor to the next line. The application moves the cursor — that is a program command. Control+N in Microsoft Word opens a new document — that is Word's command, not Windows. Your screen reader has nothing to do with moving that cursor. It simply reads aloud what the application already did. Press Enter to continue.

The third layer is your screen reader. NVDA commands are unique: they take no action in the application. They only inform. When you press NVDA+T, nothing changes in your document. The title bar does not move, no dialog opens, nothing is activated. NVDA simply announces what is already there — because sighted users can glance at the title bar any time, and you need a way to hear it. This distinction — take no action, only inform — is the heart of what makes screen reader commands different from all others. As you work through NVDA Coach, you will begin to recognize which layer each command belongs to without being told. Press Enter to begin your first lesson.

Welcome to NVDA Coach! If you have an assistive technology instructor with you right now, this is a great moment to let them know you are starting. They can sit with you, help you find keys, and answer questions as you go. If you are working on your own, that is absolutely fine. NVDA Coach is designed to guide you independently, and you can do this. If you ever feel stuck, press F2 for a hint, F1 to hear the instruction again, or F3 to skip a step and come back to it later. In this first lesson, you will learn about the NVDA modifier key. This is the special key you hold while pressing other keys to send commands to NVDA. On most keyboards it is the Insert key. On many laptops it is Caps Lock. Press Enter to continue.

NVDA+N opens the NVDA menu — your hub for all of NVDA's settings and features. Sighted users can click a menu with a mouse; NVDA+N is your equivalent. Try it now. Hold your NVDA key and press the letter N. You should hear NVDA open a menu and begin announcing items such as Preferences, Tools, and Help. Once you hear the menu open, press Escape to close it and come back here, then press Enter to continue.

Well done! You now know how to open and close the NVDA menu. NVDA+N opens the menu — it is your main hub for preferences, tools, and help. The Escape key closes menus and cancels actions, and you will use it constantly. These two shortcuts together give you full access to everything NVDA can do. Press Enter to finish this lesson.

Every window has a title bar at the top that tells you which program you are in. Sighted users can glance at the title bar at any time. NVDA+T speaks the title bar aloud — your equivalent of that glance. Press Enter to continue.

Try it now. Hold your NVDA key and press the letter T. You should hear NVDA read the title bar of the current window — something like the name of the program you are in right now. Whenever you are not sure which program you are looking at, NVDA+T will tell you immediately. It is one of the most useful commands you will ever learn. Press Enter here to continue.

Let's do that one more time to build the habit. Hold your NVDA key and press T again. Listen as NVDA reads the title bar aloud. Repetition is how keyboard commands become automatic — the more you practice, the faster each command becomes second nature. Press Enter here when you are ready to continue.

Sighted users can glance at the clock in the corner of the screen. NVDA+F12 speaks the current time without you having to navigate anywhere. NVDA can tell you the current time and date without leaving what you are doing. Press Enter to continue.

Try it now. Hold your NVDA key and press F12 — the last function key on the top row of your keyboard, usually on the far right. You should hear NVDA announce the current time. Quick tip: if you press NVDA+F12 twice in rapid succession, NVDA reads the date instead of the time. Give both a try if you like, then press Enter here to continue.

Sometimes NVDA talks too much or you want it to stop mid sentence. The Control key by itself immediately silences speech. This is called stopping speech, and it works in Narrator, JAWS, and VoiceOver as well — it is a universal screen reader convention. Press Enter to continue.

There is also a second option: the Shift key. Pressing Shift pauses NVDA's speech mid-sentence. Press Shift again and NVDA resumes reading right where it left off. This pause-and-resume behavior is specific to NVDA. It is useful when you need a brief moment to focus — for example, reading a phone number from a document while you dial it. Press Shift once to pause, then press Shift again to continue. Control stops completely. Shift pauses and resumes. Both are worth knowing. Press Enter to continue.

A note on practicing these keys: pressing Control or Shift during an active lesson would interfere with the lesson itself, so reliable in-lesson practice is not possible here. Instead, build the habit naturally. The next time NVDA reads something too long or too fast, reach for Control to stop it. When you need a moment mid-reading, try Shift to pause and resume. You will discover both reflexes quickly in real use. Press Enter to finish this lesson.

Sighted users can see which control is highlighted on screen. NVDA+Tab speaks the name, type, and state of whatever control you are currently focused on — your equivalent of that visual glance. Press Enter to continue.

A practice login form is about to open in a separate window. When it appears, press Alt+Tab to switch to it. Tab to different controls and press NVDA+Tab on each one to hear NVDA describe it. When you are done, press Alt+Tab to return here, then press NVDA+Shift+C to bring the Coach window forward, and press Enter to continue. Ready? Press Enter now to open the practice form.

Now in the practice window, Tab to any control in the login form, then press NVDA+Tab. NVDA will announce the name, type, and state of whatever control you are focused on. For example it might say 'Username edit' or 'Sign in button.' Try it on several different controls — text fields, the checkbox, and each button — to hear how NVDA describes each type. When you are done exploring, press NVDA+Shift+C to return here, then press Enter to continue.

In dialog boxes, settings windows, and forms, you press the Tab key to move forward between controls like buttons, checkboxes, and text fields. Shift+Tab moves you backwards. This is the same in every Windows program — learning it here means you can use it everywhere. Press Enter to continue.

A practice form is about to open in a separate window. When it appears, press Alt+Tab to switch to it. Press Tab to move forward through the controls. When you are done, press Alt+Tab to return here, press NVDA+Shift+C to bring the Coach window forward, and press Enter to continue. Ready? Press Enter now to open the practice form.

Switch to the practice form by pressing Alt+Tab. Once you are there, press Tab to move to the next control. NVDA will announce the name and type of each control as you land on it. Keep pressing Tab to move through the whole form. When you are ready, press NVDA+Shift+C to come back here, then press Enter to continue.
steps.hint: The Tab key is on the left side of your keyboard, above Caps Lock. Just press it by itself. Each time you press Tab, NVDA announces the control you moved to.
steps.successMessage: You moved to the next control. NVDA announced what you landed on.

steps.instruction: Now in the practice form, press Shift+Tab to move back to the previous control. Hold Shift and press Tab. NVDA will announce the control you return to. Tab goes forward, Shift+Tab goes back. Press NVDA+Shift+C to return here when ready, then press Enter to continue.
steps.hint: Hold the Shift key and press Tab. Shift is the large wide key just above Ctrl on either side of the keyboard.
steps.successMessage: You moved backwards. Tab goes forward, Shift+Tab goes back. These two keys will carry you through most dialog boxes and forms.

steps.instruction: Once you have Tabbed to a button or control, you need to activate it. Enter activates buttons and default actions. Space toggles checkboxes on and off. These keys work the same way in every Windows program. Press Enter to continue.

A practice window with checkboxes and buttons is about to open. When it appears, press Alt+Tab to switch to it. When you are done, press Alt+Tab to return here, press NVDA+Shift+C to bring the Coach window forward, and press Enter to continue. Ready? Press Enter now to open the practice window.

Switch to the practice window with Alt+Tab. Tab to one of the checkboxes labeled 'Enable screen reader tips' or 'Show practice hints', then press the Space bar to check or uncheck it. NVDA will announce whether the box is now checked or unchecked. When you are done, press NVDA+Shift+C to return here, then press Enter to continue.
steps.hint: The Space bar is the long key at the bottom center of your keyboard. Make sure you have Tabbed to a checkbox first.
steps.successMessage: Space bar pressed. Space is used to check or uncheck checkboxes and to activate some buttons.

steps.instruction: Now in the practice window, Tab to one of the buttons — 'Play a beep sound', 'Say hello', or 'Show keyboard tip' — and press Enter to activate it. NVDA will announce what happened. When done, press NVDA+Shift+C to return here, then press Enter to continue.
steps.hint: Enter is the large key on the right side of the main keyboard area. Make sure you have Tabbed to a button first.
steps.successMessage: Enter key pressed. Enter activates buttons and follows links. Between Tab, Space, and Enter, you can navigate almost any dialog box.

steps.instruction: When you are in a document or text area, you can have NVDA read the line where your cursor is sitting. NVDA+Up Arrow reads the current line — useful whenever you want to confirm exactly where you are in a document. A practice text area is included right in the next step. Press Enter to continue.

Arrow down into the practice text area below to move your cursor through the lines. Then press NVDA+Up Arrow to have NVDA read the current line aloud. Move to a different line with Up or Down Arrow and press NVDA+Up Arrow again to hear each one. This works in any document, email, or text area. Press Enter to continue when done.
steps.practiceText:
The quick brown fox jumps over the lazy dog.
Screen readers open the digital world to everyone.
NVDA is free and open source software.
Practice builds the habit of screen reader navigation.
Arrow keys move through text one step at a time.
Control plus arrows jump by whole words at once.
Home and End move to a line boundary.
Control plus Home or End jumps to document extremes.
This is the last line of the practice area.

steps.instruction: NVDA+1 toggles Input Help mode. When it is on, pressing any key tells you what that key does in NVDA — without actually doing anything. It is a completely safe way to explore your keyboard, and it is unique to NVDA. Press Enter to continue.

Try it now. Hold your NVDA key and press the number 1 from the top row of your keyboard — not the numpad, but the number row just above the letter keys. You should hear NVDA say 'Input help on.' While Input Help is active, you can press any key and NVDA will describe what that key does without actually performing the action. Press Enter here to continue.

Now press NVDA+1 again to turn Input Help back off. Hold your NVDA key and press the number 1. You should hear NVDA say 'Input help off.' Whenever you forget what a key does in the future, turn on Input Help, press the key to hear its description, and then turn Input Help back off. It is like having a built-in cheat sheet that is always available to you. Press Enter here to continue.

NVDA comes with a complete user guide that you can open at any time. The user guide is found in the NVDA menu under Help. It is a great reference when you want to learn more on your own. Press Enter to continue.

Try it now. Hold your NVDA key and press N to open the NVDA menu. You should hear NVDA announce the menu and its items. From here, in real use, you would press the Down Arrow to move through items, navigate to Help, and then select User Guide to read the full documentation at any time. For now, just listen to the menu open, then press Escape to close it and come back here. Press Enter to continue.

Excellent work — you have completed the Getting Started chapter! You now know how to identify the three categories of commands, and you have practiced the essential NVDA commands that will carry you through daily use: your NVDA key, reading the title bar and time, stopping speech, finding your focus, navigating with Tab, activating controls, reading lines, using Input Help, and opening the user guide. Practice a little every day and these commands will become automatic. When you are ready for more, press NVDA+Shift+C to open the lesson picker and start the next chapter: Reading and Moving Through Text. Press Enter to finish.
